content:
1) you can build it by hand understanding what you do, you should think that the kernel runs in a forward pass with new data, it should not replay itself during benchmarking for example
2) if using llms, to can learn about the exploits that the LLMs can do to reward-hack, for example from here: https://deep-reinforce.com/defense_kernel_hack.html
3) Apply common sense to submissions, it's highly unlikely that you will 4x improve aiters performance by some simple code addition, really smart people built those kernels. In general if something looks too good to be true, you should verify so you understand what is going on

content: benchmarks can be hacked. i didn't look at benchmarking logic here but for example if the same tensor is provide multiple times we can cache it and thus avoid reloading it. this is something that of course doesn't make much sense in production because its unlikely one and the same set of tensors will be encountered multiple times in a row

content:
Thanks, @daniel huang ..looks like it was me. 
What I was doing wrong:
Inside my custom_kernel, I was calling .contiguous() and .view(torch.uint8) on the input tensors to prep them for Triton. Because Python passes tensors by reference, this mutated the underlying memory strides and C++ metadata.
When the eval.py harness subsequently passed those same mutated tensors into the aiter reference kernel to check for mathematical correctness, Aiter couldn't handle the new strides/dtypes and triggered a fatal ROCm hardware fault (rocdevice.cpp :3580: Callback: Queue aborting). Because the GPU queue died, the host eval.py just hung on synchronization until the hard 420s CI timeout killed the job.
referenceMessage: I am not sure what happened, but other submissions about mla and moe work...
